tagline: Calculate GST, HST, or PST by province — add or remove sales tax from any amount
keywords: GST calculator, HST calculator, PST, Canada sales tax, provincial tax, GST HST by province

faq.question: What is the difference between GST and HST?
faq.answer: GST (Goods and Services Tax) is a 5% federal tax. HST (Harmonized Sales Tax) combines GST with provincial sales tax into a single rate. Ontario charges 13% HST, while Atlantic provinces charge 15% HST.

faq.question: Which provinces charge PST separately?
faq.answer: British Columbia (7% PST), Saskatchewan (6% PST), Manitoba (7% RST), and Quebec (9.975% QST) charge provincial tax separately from the 5% federal GST.

faq.question: Which provinces have no provincial sales tax?
faq.answer: Alberta, Northwest Territories, Nunavut, and Yukon only charge the 5% federal GST with no provincial sales tax.

faq.question: Can I claim GST/HST back?
faq.answer: Businesses registered for GST/HST can claim Input Tax Credits (ITCs) to recover GST/HST paid on business expenses. Low-income individuals may qualify for the GST/HST credit, paid quarterly.

formulas: GST = Amount x GST rate (5% federal, or combined HST rate)
PST/QST = Amount x Provincial rate (varies by province)
Total with tax = Amount x (1 + combined rate)
Remove tax: Subtotal = Total / (1 + combined rate)

tagline: Calculate your RRSP contribution room, tax savings, and projected retirement savings
keywords: RRSP calculator, RRSP contribution room, RRSP tax savings, registered retirement savings plan, RRSP deduction

faq.question: What is the RRSP contribution limit for 2024?
faq.answer: The 2024 RRSP contribution limit is 18% of your previous year's earned income, up to a maximum of $31,560. Unused contribution room carries forward from previous years.

faq.question: How does an RRSP reduce my taxes?
faq.answer: RRSP contributions are tax-deductible. If you contribute $10,000 and your marginal tax rate is 30%, you save $3,000 in taxes. You pay tax later when you withdraw in retirement, ideally at a lower rate.

faq.question: When is the RRSP contribution deadline?
faq.answer: You have until 60 days after December 31 (typically March 1) to make contributions that count for the previous tax year. For the 2024 tax year, the deadline is March 3, 2025.

faq.question: RRSP vs TFSA — which should I prioritize?
faq.answer: If your current marginal tax rate is higher than your expected rate in retirement, the RRSP typically provides more benefit. If your rate will be similar or higher in retirement, the TFSA may be better since withdrawals are tax-free.

formulas: Contribution room = 18% of earned income, max $31,560 (2024)
Tax savings = Contribution x Marginal tax rate
After-tax cost = Contribution - Tax savings
Projected balance = Current balance compounded + annual contributions compounded

tagline: Calculate your combined federal and provincial income tax for any Canadian province using 2024 brackets
keywords: Canada income tax, provincial tax calculator, federal tax brackets, Ontario tax, BC tax, Alberta tax, Quebec tax

faq.question: What are the 2024 federal income tax brackets?
Which province has the lowest income tax?
faq.answer: Alberta has the lowest provincial rates with a flat 10% on the first $142,292. Provinces like Quebec and Nova Scotia have the highest combined rates, with Quebec's top rate at 25.75%.

faq.question: What is the basic personal amount?
faq.answer: The 2024 federal basic personal amount is $15,705, meaning the first $15,705 of income is effectively tax-free at the federal level. Each province has its own basic personal amount.

faq.question: How does the Quebec tax system differ?
faq.answer: Quebec collects its own income tax separately (not through the CRA) and has its own brackets. Quebec residents receive a federal tax abatement of 16.5% and do not pay the federal portion on some credits.

formulas: Basic personal amount: federal $15,705 (2024)
Combined marginal rate = Federal marginal rate + Provincial marginal rate
Effective rate = Total tax / Taxable income x 100

tagline: Project your Tax-Free Savings Account growth and see the tax-free advantage over a regular account
keywords: TFSA calculator, tax-free savings account, TFSA contribution room, TFSA growth, TFSA vs RRSP, TFSA limit

faq.question: What is the 2024 TFSA contribution limit?
faq.answer: The 2024 annual TFSA contribution limit is $7,000. If you have been eligible since the TFSA was introduced in 2009, your cumulative room is $95,000. Unused room carries forward indefinitely.

faq.question: Are TFSA withdrawals tax-free?
faq.answer: Yes. All withdrawals from a TFSA, including investment gains, are completely tax-free. Withdrawals also do not affect government benefits like OAS or GIS.

faq.question: Can I hold stocks and ETFs in my TFSA?
faq.answer: Yes. A TFSA can hold cash, GICs, bonds, mutual funds, ETFs, stocks, and other qualified investments. Using growth investments in a TFSA maximizes the tax-free benefit.

faq.question: What happens if I over-contribute to my TFSA?
faq.answer: Over-contributions are subject to a 1% per month penalty tax on the excess amount. Track your available room carefully through your CRA My Account.

formulas: TFSA contribution limit: $7,000/year (2024), cumulative $95,000 since 2009
Projected balance = current balance compounded + monthly contributions compounded
Tax saved = Investment growth x Marginal tax rate
Monthly income estimate = Balance x 4% / 12

tagline: Calculate your CPP contributions and estimate your monthly retirement pension benefit
keywords: CPP calculator, Canada Pension Plan, CPP contributions, CPP retirement benefit, CPP2, CPP estimate

faq.question: How much is the maximum CPP benefit in 2024?
faq.answer: The maximum monthly CPP retirement pension at age 65 in 2024 is $1,364.60. The average payment is about $815.00. Your actual benefit depends on your contribution history and when you start receiving it.

faq.question: What is CPP2?
faq.answer: CPP2, starting in 2024, is a second enhanced CPP contribution on earnings between the first ceiling ($68,500) and the second ceiling ($73,200). The rate is 4% each for employee and employer. It will provide additional retirement benefits.

faq.question: When should I start taking CPP?
faq.answer: You can start CPP as early as 60 (with a 0.6%/month reduction, or 36% at 60) or delay until 70 (with a 0.7%/month increase, or 42% at 70). If you expect to live past 74, delaying to 70 typically provides more total lifetime income.

faq.question: Do self-employed people pay double CPP?
faq.answer: Yes. Self-employed individuals pay both the employee and employer portions of CPP, totaling 11.9% of pensionable earnings (5.95% x 2). The CPP2 rate is also doubled for self-employed: 8% total.

formulas: CPP1 contribution = (Earnings up to $68,500 - $3,500 basic exemption) x 5.95%
CPP2 contribution = Earnings between $68,500 and $73,200 x 4%
Self-employed: double both CPP1 and CPP2 rates
Early/late adjustment: -0.6%/month before 65, +0.7%/month after 65

tagline: See if you qualify under the B-20 mortgage stress test using the qualifying rate
keywords: mortgage stress test, B-20, qualifying rate, Canada mortgage, GDS, TDS, mortgage qualification

faq.question: What is the mortgage stress test?
faq.answer: The B-20 stress test requires borrowers to qualify at the higher of: their contract rate + 2%, or the Bank of Canada's qualifying rate (currently 5.25%). This ensures you can afford payments if rates rise.

faq.question: What are the GDS and TDS limits?
faq.answer: GDS (Gross Debt Service) ratio must be under 39% — this includes mortgage, property tax, and heating costs. TDS (Total Debt Service) must be under 44% — this adds all other debt payments.

faq.question: How much down payment do I need?
faq.answer: Minimum 5% for homes up to $500,000. For homes $500,000-$999,999: 5% on first $500K plus 10% on remainder. Homes $1M+ require 20% minimum. Below 20%, CMHC mortgage insurance is required.

faq.question: Does the stress test apply to renewals?
faq.answer: The stress test only applies when obtaining a new mortgage or switching lenders. Renewing with your current lender does not require a new stress test, though some lenders may still apply it.

formulas: Qualifying rate = Higher of (contract rate + 2%) or 5.25%
GDS ratio = (Mortgage + Property tax + Heating) / Gross monthly income x 100
TDS ratio = (GDS costs + Other debts) / Gross monthly income x 100

tagline: Calculate your EI premiums and estimate your benefit if you lose your job
keywords: EI calculator, employment insurance, EI premiums, EI benefits, Canada EI, unemployment insurance Canada

faq.question: What is the 2024 EI premium rate?
faq.answer: The 2024 employee EI premium rate is $1.66 per $100 of insurable earnings (1.66%), or $1.312 in Quebec. The employer pays 1.4 times the employee rate. Maximum insurable earnings are $63,200, so the maximum annual employee premium is $1,049.12.

faq.question: How much will I receive on EI?
faq.answer: EI pays 55% of your average insurable weekly earnings, up to a maximum of $668 per week in 2024. The number of weeks depends on hours worked and the regional unemployment rate.

faq.question: How many hours do I need to qualify for EI?
faq.answer: You generally need 420-700 insurable hours in the past 52 weeks, depending on your region's unemployment rate. Areas with higher unemployment require fewer hours to qualify.

faq.question: Can self-employed people get EI?
faq.answer: Self-employed individuals can opt into EI special benefits (maternity, parental, sickness, compassionate care) but not regular benefits. You pay the employee premium but the employer portion is waived.

formulas: Employee premium = Insurable earnings x 1.66% (1.312% in Quebec), max $63,200
Employer premium = Employee premium x 1.4
Weekly benefit = 55% of average weekly insurable earnings, max $668/week
Benefit weeks vary by hours worked and regional unemployment rate

tagline: Calculate your monthly Canada Child Benefit payments based on income and number of children
keywords: Canada Child Benefit, CCB calculator, child tax benefit, child benefit by income, CCB payment

faq.question: What is the maximum Canada Child Benefit?
faq.answer: For 2024-25, the maximum annual CCB is $7,437 per child under 6 and $6,275 per child aged 6-17. These amounts are tax-free and paid monthly. The benefit is reduced based on family net income.

faq.question: At what income does the CCB start reducing?
faq.answer: The CCB begins reducing when adjusted family net income exceeds $36,502. The reduction rate depends on the number of children: 7% for one child, 13.5% for two, 19% for three, and 23% for four or more.

faq.question: Is the Canada Child Benefit taxable?
faq.answer: No. CCB payments are completely tax-free. They do not need to be reported as income on your tax return and do not affect other income-tested benefits.

faq.question: When are CCB payments made?
faq.answer: CCB is paid monthly, usually around the 20th of each month. The benefit year runs from July to June, based on the previous year's tax return. You must file a tax return to receive CCB.

formulas: Max benefit: $7,437/child under 6, $6,275/child 6-17 (2024-25)
First reduction: income over $36,502 x rate (7% for 1 child, 13.5% for 2, etc.)
Second reduction: income over $79,087 x rate (3.2% for 1 child, 5.7% for 2, etc.)
Monthly payment = (Max benefit - Reduction) / 12

tagline: Calculate capital gains tax with the 50% inclusion rate and new 66.67% rate for gains over $250,000
keywords: Canada capital gains tax, capital gains inclusion rate, capital gains calculator, 50% inclusion, 66.67% inclusion

faq.question: What is the capital gains inclusion rate in Canada?
faq.answer: As of June 25, 2024, the first $250,000 of capital gains per year is included at 50% (you pay tax on half). Capital gains above $250,000 are included at 66.67% (two-thirds). For corporations and trusts, the 66.67% rate applies to all gains.

faq.question: How are capital losses applied?
faq.answer: Capital losses can only be applied against capital gains, not other income. Net capital losses can be carried back 3 years or carried forward indefinitely. Only 50% of losses are deductible (the allowable capital loss).

faq.question: Is a principal residence subject to capital gains tax?
faq.answer: No. Your principal residence is generally exempt from capital gains tax under the Principal Residence Exemption. You must designate the property and report the sale on your tax return.

faq.question: What is the adjusted cost base (ACB)?
faq.answer: The ACB is your original purchase price plus any acquisition costs (legal fees, commissions) and capital improvements. It does not include maintenance costs or mortgage interest. Accurate ACB records are essential for calculating gains.

formulas: Capital Gain = Proceeds - Adjusted Cost Base - Selling Expenses
First $250,000: Taxable gain = 50% inclusion x Capital gain
Above $250,000: Taxable gain = 66.67% inclusion x Capital gain
Tax = Taxable capital gain x Marginal tax rate

tagline: Calculate land transfer tax by province for your property purchase, including first-time buyer rebates
keywords: land transfer tax, property transfer tax, LTT calculator, Ontario land transfer tax, BC property transfer tax, Toronto municipal tax

faq.question: What is land transfer tax?
faq.answer: Land transfer tax (LTT) is a provincial tax paid when you purchase property. Rates vary by province and are based on the purchase price. Ontario, BC, Quebec, Manitoba, and Atlantic provinces all charge LTT. Alberta and Saskatchewan do not have a land transfer tax.

faq.question: Does Toronto charge additional land transfer tax?
faq.answer: Yes. Toronto is the only city in Canada that charges a municipal land transfer tax on top of the provincial LTT. The Toronto municipal LTT roughly mirrors the Ontario provincial rates, effectively doubling the tax for Toronto buyers.

faq.question: What rebates are available for first-time buyers?
faq.answer: Ontario offers up to $4,000 rebate on provincial LTT and $4,475 on Toronto municipal LTT for first-time buyers. BC offers a full exemption on homes up to $835,000. Other provinces may have similar programs.

faq.question: What is the foreign buyer tax?
faq.answer: Ontario charges a 25% Non-Resident Speculation Tax and BC charges a 20% Foreign Buyer Tax on property purchases by non-Canadian citizens or permanent residents in designated areas.

formulas: Ontario LTT: 0.5% on first $55k, 1% on $55k-$250k, 1.5% on $250k-$400k, 2% on $400k-$2M, 2.5% over $2M
BC PTT: 1% on first $200k, 2% on $200k-$2M, 3% on $2M-$3M, 5% over $3M
Toronto Municipal LTT mirrors Ontario provincial rates (additional charge)
First-time buyer rebates: ON up to $4,000, BC full exemption up to $835,000
